title: Lookup Gate
objective: Pick the structure with O(1) average key lookup.
hint: Think buckets and keys.
question: Which data structure offers O(1) average-case lookup by key?
story_fail: Hashing trades order for speed.
explanation: A hash map maps keys to buckets via a hash function, giving O(1) average lookup at the cost of ordering.

title: Halving Search
objective: Name the complexity of binary search.
hint: Each step halves the search space.
question: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array of n items?
story_fail: Each comparison removes half the candidates.
explanation: Binary search discards half the remaining elements each step, giving O(log n).

title: Two Pointer Trace
objective: Predict the output of a two-pointer sum check.
hint: Pointers start at both ends and move inward.
question: nums = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9], target = 12. Two pointers start at index 0 and 4 and move toward each other while summing the ends. What pair (as two numbers separated by a space) is returned?
story_fail: 1+9=10 too low, move the left pointer right; 3+9=12.
explanation: Two-pointer scanning on a sorted array moves the low pointer up when the sum is too small and the high pointer down when too large.

title: Reverse Signal
objective: Write a Go snippet that reverses a string by runes.
hint: Convert to []rune, swap from both ends.
question: Write a Go function reverse(s string) string that returns s reversed, safe for unicode (use a rune slice).
answer: func reverse(s string) string r []rune(s) for i j 0 len(r)-1 i j r[i] r[j] r[j] r[i] return string(r)
story_fail: Convert to []rune first; byte-reversal breaks multi-byte characters.
explanation: Go strings are UTF-8 byte sequences; reversing bytes can corrupt multi-byte runes, so convert to []rune and swap symmetrically.

title: Level Order
objective: Name the traversal that visits nodes level by level.
hint: It uses a queue, not a stack.
question: Which traversal visits a binary tree's nodes level by level (top to bottom, left to right)?
story_fail: Queues give FIFO order — that's level order, i.e. BFS.
explanation: BFS uses a FIFO queue so nodes are dequeued in the order they were discovered, producing level-by-level traversal.

title: Window Slide
objective: Fill the missing line in a sliding-window sum.
hint: Subtract the element leaving the window, add the one entering.
story_fail: Drop the element that just left the window: nums[i-k].
explanation: A fixed-size sliding window keeps a running sum by adding the new right edge and removing the element k positions back.

title: Bracket Trap
objective: Spot the bug in a stack-based bracket checker.
hint: What happens when the stack is empty on a closing bracket?
answer: no empty stack check before popping
accepted: index out of range on empty stack
story_fail: A closing bracket with an empty stack indexes stack[-1] and panics — guard with len(stack) == 0 first.
explanation: Always check whether the stack is empty before reading its top; an unmatched closing bracket would otherwise cause an out-of-range panic.

title: Memo Gate
objective: Name the complexity of memoized Fibonacci.
hint: Each subproblem is solved once and cached.
question: What is the time complexity of computing fib(n) with top-down memoization (recursion + cache)?
story_fail: There are only n distinct subproblems; caching makes each one O(1) after the first solve.
explanation: Memoization collapses the exponential naive recursion to one computation per distinct subproblem — n of them — giving O(n).

title: Post-Group Filter
objective: Pick the clause that filters after grouping.
hint: WHERE runs before grouping; this one runs after.
question: Which SQL clause filters rows after GROUP BY has produced aggregated groups?
story_fail: WHERE filters rows pre-aggregation; HAVING filters the resulting groups.
explanation: HAVING applies predicates to aggregated group results, while WHERE applies to individual rows before grouping.

title: Salary Filter
objective: Write a query selecting high earners.
hint: SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE salary > 50000
question: Write a query that selects the name and salary of employees from the employees table earning more than 50000.
answer: select name salary from employees where salary 50000
accepted: select name salary from employees where salary 50000
story_fail: select name, salary from employees where salary > 50000;
explanation: WHERE filters individual employee rows by a numeric comparison before any rows are returned.

title: Named Subquery
objective: Pick the construct for a temporary named result set.
hint: It starts with WITH.
question: Which SQL construct lets you define a temporary named result set usable within a single query?
story_fail: A CTE, written with WITH name AS (...), names a subquery for reuse in the main query.
explanation: CTEs (WITH clauses) give a readable name to a subquery, scoped to the statement that follows.

title: Count Trace
objective: Predict the output of a filtered COUNT query.
hint: Count only rows where x > 5.
question: Table t has column x with rows [3, 6, 9, 2, 7]. What number does SELECT COUNT(*) FROM t WHERE x > 5 return?
story_fail: 6, 9, and 7 are greater than 5 — three rows.
explanation: WHERE x > 5 keeps rows 6, 9, and 7; COUNT(*) then reports how many rows survived the filter.

title: Department Rank
objective: Write a window-function ranking query.
hint: RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...)
question: Write a query that ranks employees by salary (highest first) within each department using a window function. Select department, name, and the rank.
answer: select department name rank() over (partition by department order by salary desc) from employees
accepted: select department name rank over partition by department order by salary desc from employees
story_fail: select department, name, rank() over (partition by department order by salary desc) from employees;
explanation: PARTITION BY resets the ranking per department while ORDER BY salary DESC ranks within each partition — no GROUP BY collapsing needed.

title: Outer Match
objective: Pick the join that keeps unmatched left rows.
hint: Unmatched right-side columns become NULL.
question: Which join returns all rows from the left table plus matched rows from the right, filling unmatched right columns with NULL?
story_fail: LEFT JOIN preserves every left-table row regardless of a match on the right.
explanation: LEFT JOIN keeps every row from the left table; rows with no right-side match get NULLs in the right columns.

title: Spread Measure
objective: Pick the measure of average squared deviation.
hint: Its square root is the standard deviation.
question: Which measure captures the average squared deviation from the mean?
story_fail: Variance averages squared distances from the mean; its square root is standard deviation.
explanation: Variance = E[(X - mean)^2]; squaring keeps deviations positive and weights larger gaps more heavily.

title: Coin Pair
objective: Compute a probability mentally.
hint: HT and TH both count as 'exactly one head'.
question: A fair coin is flipped twice. What is the probability of getting exactly one head? Answer as a decimal (e.g. 0.5).
story_win: Outcomes counted cleanly: HT, TH out of 4.
story_fail: Two of the four equally likely outcomes (HT, TH) have exactly one head: 2/4 = 0.5.
explanation: Of the four equally likely outcomes HH, HT, TH, TT, exactly two have one head, giving probability 0.5.

title: Bell Curve
objective: Name the symmetric bell-shaped distribution.
hint: Defined fully by its mean and variance.
question: Which distribution is symmetric, bell-shaped, and fully described by its mean and variance?
story_fail: The Normal (Gaussian) distribution is the symmetric bell curve parameterized by mean and variance.
explanation: The normal distribution N(mu, sigma^2) is symmetric around its mean and its shape is fully determined by mean and variance.

title: Significance Call
objective: Interpret a small p-value in an A/B test.
hint: A small p-value argues against the null.
question: In an A/B test, a p-value below the chosen significance threshold most directly suggests you should...
story_fail: A small p-value means the observed difference is unlikely under the null, so you reject it.
explanation: The p-value is the probability of seeing data this extreme if the null were true; a small value is evidence against the null.

title: Quick Mean
objective: Compute a mean mentally.
hint: Sum the values, divide by the count.
question: Dataset: 2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9. What is the mean? Answer as a number.
story_fail: Sum = 40, count = 8, mean = 40/8 = 5.
explanation: The arithmetic mean is the sum of values divided by how many there are: 40 / 8 = 5.

title: Interval Meaning
objective: Pick the correct reading of a confidence interval.
hint: It is about the procedure's long-run reliability, not a single guarantee.
question: What does a 95% confidence interval for a parameter best represent?
choices: The exact value of the parameter
A range produced by a procedure that captures the true value about 95% of the time
The probability the null hypothesis is true
answer: a range produced by a procedure that captures the true value about 95 of the time
story_win: Interval read with the right caveat.
story_fail: It describes the long-run capture rate of the estimation procedure, not a 95% chance for this one interval.
explanation: Confidence level describes how often the interval-construction procedure would capture the true parameter across repeated samples.

title: Memorized Training
objective: Name the failure mode of memorizing training data.
hint: Great on train, poor on unseen data.
question: A model performs great on training data but poorly on new data. What is this called?
story_fail: Memorizing noise in training data instead of learning general patterns is overfitting.
explanation: Overfitting happens when a model captures noise specific to the training set rather than the underlying signal.

title: Bias Side
objective: Map high bias / low variance to a fitting regime.
hint: A model that's too simple misses the pattern entirely.
question: A model with high bias and low variance is best described as...
story_fail: High bias means the model's assumptions are too rigid to capture the pattern — underfitting.
explanation: High bias = strong simplifying assumptions that systematically miss the true pattern, regardless of which training sample is used.

title: Predicted Positives
objective: Pin down what precision measures.
hint: It looks at the predicted-positive set.
question: Precision answers which question?
choices: Of the items predicted positive, how many are actually positive?
Of the items that are actually positive, how many did we find?
What fraction of all predictions were correct?
How fast does the model train?
answer: of the items predicted positive how many are actually positive
story_fail: Precision = true positives / (true positives + false positives) — it scrutinizes the predicted-positive set.
explanation: Precision focuses on the reliability of positive predictions: how many flagged items were genuinely positive.

title: Actual Positives
objective: Pin down what recall measures.
hint: It looks at the actual-positive set.
question: Recall answers which question?
choices: Of the items predicted positive, how many are actually positive?
Of the items that are actually positive, how many did the model find?
What fraction of all predictions were correct?
How fast does the model train?
answer: of the items that are actually positive how many did the model find
story_fail: Recall = true positives / (true positives + false negatives) — it measures how much of the real positive set was captured.
explanation: Recall measures completeness: out of everything that is truly positive, what share the model successfully flagged.

title: Future Peek
objective: Define data leakage.
hint: It involves information the model shouldn't have at prediction time.
question: What is data leakage in machine learning?
choices: Using future or target-derived information during training that won't be available at prediction time
Losing rows during a pipeline crash
answer: using future or target derived information during training that wont be available at prediction time
story_fail: Leakage means training signal contains information that peeks at the answer or the future, inflating offline metrics.
explanation: Leakage gives the model access — directly or indirectly — to the target or to future information, producing unrealistically good validation scores that collapse in production.

title: Raw to Useful
objective: State the goal of feature engineering.
hint: It's about shaping inputs, not the algorithm.
question: Feature engineering primarily aims to...
choices: Transform raw data into informative inputs the model can learn from
Make the training code run faster
Increase the size of the dataset
Hide sensitive columns from the model
answer: transform raw data into informative inputs the model can learn from
story_fail: Feature engineering reshapes, combines, or encodes raw fields into representations the model can actually exploit.
explanation: Good features expose the structure in raw data — via encoding, scaling, combining, or extracting — so the model can learn the relationship more easily.

title: Pipeline Acronym
objective: Expand the ETL acronym.
hint: Three verbs describing a data pipeline's stages.
question: ETL stands for...
story_fail: ETL = Extract from sources, Transform into the target shape, Load into the destination.
explanation: ETL describes the classic pipeline shape: pull data out, reshape/clean it, then write it into the destination store.

title: Lookup Boost
objective: State why you'd index a column.
hint: It trades write cost and storage for read speed.
question: Why would you add an index to a frequently filtered database column?
choices: To speed up lookups and filters on that column
To shrink the table's storage to zero
To automatically clean bad data in that column
To encrypt the column at rest
answer: to speed up lookups and filters on that column
story_fail: An index builds an ordered structure over a column so the engine can avoid scanning every row.
explanation: Indexes let the database jump directly to matching rows instead of scanning the whole table, at the cost of extra storage and slower writes.

title: Slice by Date
objective: State the main benefit of partitioning by date.
hint: Queries can skip irrelevant slices entirely.
question: Partitioning a large table by date primarily helps query performance because it enables...
choices: Pruning scans to only the relevant date partitions
answer: pruning scans to only the relevant date partitions
story_fail: A query for 'last 7 days' can skip every other partition instead of scanning the whole table — that's partition pruning.
explanation: When the query's filter aligns with the partition key, the engine can skip reading partitions that can't contain matching rows.

title: Quality Axis
objective: Identify a recognized data-quality dimension.
hint: Think about whether values are present, not how they're stored.
question: Which of these is a standard data-quality dimension?
story_fail: Completeness — are required values present — is one of the classic data-quality dimensions, alongside accuracy, consistency, timeliness, and validity.
explanation: Completeness asks whether expected fields and records are actually present, a core axis used to measure trustworthiness of a dataset.

title: Event Log Shape
objective: Choose a storage model for immutable, time-queried events.
hint: Immutable + queried by time range is a strong hint.
question: You must store millions of immutable event logs, queried mostly by time range. Which storage shape fits best?
choices: Star schema with slowly changing dimensions
answer: a time partitioned append only table
story_win: Event stream shaped for its access pattern.
story_fail: Immutable, time-range-queried, high-volume events fit an append-only table partitioned by time, enabling partition pruning and cheap writes.
explanation: Matching the storage shape to the access pattern — append-mostly, time-range reads — lets partitioning do most of the performance work for free.

title: Safe Retry
objective: Name the property that prevents partial/duplicated writes on retry.
hint: Running the same operation twice should be safe.
question: A pipeline job fails halfway through writing and gets retried from the start. What property of the write step prevents partial or duplicated data?
story_win: Retry landed safely on the same end state.
story_fail: An idempotent write (e.g. upsert by key, or write-then-atomic-rename) produces the same end state no matter how many times it runs.
explanation: Idempotent writes — keyed upserts, atomic replace, dedup-by-id — make retries safe because re-running them converges to the same final state.

title: Replace in Place
objective: Pick the conventionally idempotent full-replace verb.
hint: Calling it twice with the same body should leave the same result.
question: Which HTTP method is conventionally idempotent and used to fully replace a resource?
story_fail: PUT replaces a resource wholesale; repeating it with the same payload yields the same end state — that's idempotency.
explanation: By convention PUT means 'store this representation at this URL', so repeating the same PUT produces the same final resource state.

title: Hot Path Shield
objective: State the main benefit of a read cache.
hint: It sits between clients and the database for frequently read data.
question: Adding a cache in front of a database for frequently read data primarily helps by...
choices: Reducing read latency and database load for hot data
Replacing the need for indexes entirely
answer: reducing read latency and database load for hot data
story_win: Hot path shielded from the database.
story_fail: A cache absorbs repeat reads of popular data so the database only handles misses — faster responses, lighter load.
explanation: Caches trade a small risk of staleness for big wins on latency and database load by serving hot reads from memory.

title: Buffer Between
objective: Explain the role of a message queue.
hint: It sits between producers and consumers.
question: Placing a message queue between two services mainly helps you...
choices: Decouple producer and consumer and absorb load spikes
Force every call to be synchronous
Eliminate the need for any database
answer: decouple producer and consumer and absorb load spikes
story_win: Spike absorbed without toppling the consumer.
story_fail: A queue lets the producer keep moving even if the consumer is slow or briefly down — decoupling plus a buffer for bursts.
explanation: Queues let producers and consumers run at their own pace; the buffer smooths bursts and survives temporary consumer outages.

title: Flexible Events
objective: Choose a database family for flexible, fast-write, scaling needs.
hint: Schema flexibility plus horizontal scale is the key signal.
question: You need a flexible schema, fast writes, and horizontal scale for unstructured event data. Which fits best?
story_win: Schema flexed without breaking the pipeline.
story_fail: Document/NoSQL stores trade strict relational guarantees for schema flexibility and easier horizontal scaling — a good fit here.
explanation: Document stores accept varying shapes per record and are designed to shard across nodes, matching 'flexible schema + scale' requirements.

title: Add More Boxes
objective: Define horizontal scaling.
hint: It's about the count of machines, not their size.
question: Horizontal scaling means...
choices: Adding more machines or instances to share the load
Adding more RAM or CPU to a single machine
Reducing the number of running servers
answer: adding more machines or instances to share the load
story_fail: Horizontal = scale out (more nodes); vertical = scale up (bigger node).
explanation: Horizontal scaling distributes load across additional instances, while vertical scaling grows the resources of a single instance.

title: Scan At Scale
objective: Predict the symptom of an unindexed full-table scan under growth.
hint: Work grows with table size, and the table is growing.
question: An API endpoint runs a full table scan per request. As traffic and data both grow 100x, what's the most likely user-facing symptom?
story_win: Bottleneck predicted before it paged anyone.
story_fail: Scan cost grows with table size; multiplying both rows and request volume compounds the slowdown into timeouts.
explanation: A full scan's cost scales with table size; doing it more often on a bigger table multiplies latency until requests start timing out.

title: Main Gate
objective: Identify the runnable command package.
hint: Executables start in a special package.
question: A Go program that builds into a runnable command must use which package name for its entry package?
story_win: The command has a valid entry point.
story_fail: A runnable Go command uses package main and a func main entry point.
explanation: Engineering concept: keep the command entry small. package main wires flags, IO, and dependencies; reusable logic should live in smaller packages.

title: Error Contract
objective: Choose Go's normal error-handling shape.
hint: Errors are values returned by functions.
question: Which shape best matches idiomatic Go when a function can fail?
choices: return a value and an error
store the error in a global variable
ignore the failure and log later
answer: return a value and an error
story_win: Failure became part of the contract.
story_fail: In Go, ordinary failures are returned as error values and checked by the caller.
explanation: Engineering concept: explicit errors make control flow visible. The caller can retry, wrap, log, or stop instead of being surprised by hidden exceptions.

title: Table Test Loop
objective: Fill the loop header for a table-driven test.
hint: Iterate over the cases slice with range.
story_win: The test scales by adding rows, not copy-paste.
story_fail: Use range over the cases slice: for _, tc := range cases.
explanation: Engineering concept: table-driven tests keep behavior examples compact, make edge cases cheap to add, and reduce duplicated test code.

title: Small Interface
objective: Choose the easier-to-test dependency boundary.
hint: Consumer-owned interfaces should be tiny.
question: A service only needs to save one record. Which dependency boundary is usually easier to test?
choices: Accept a huge concrete database client
Accept an interface with Save(record) error
Use a package global client everywhere
Open a new production connection inside every function
answer: accept an interface with save record error
story_win: The boundary is small enough to fake in tests.
story_fail: A tiny consumer-side interface expresses exactly what the code needs and no more.
explanation: Engineering concept: small interfaces reduce coupling. Tests can provide a simple fake, while production can pass the real storage implementation.

title: Cancel Signal
objective: Pick the standard cancellation carrier.
hint: It also carries deadlines.
question: Which standard Go type should long-running request work receive so it can stop on cancellation or deadline?
story_win: The goroutine has a stop path.
story_fail: Pass context.Context through request-scoped work and select on ctx.Done() when waiting.
explanation: Engineering concept: production systems need cancellation. Context prevents leaked goroutines and lets timeouts propagate through call chains.

title: Release Line
objective: Pick the minimum repeatable Go validation line.
hint: Format, analyze, test, then build.
question: Before shipping a Go CLI change, which validation sequence is the strongest baseline?
choices: Only run the binary once manually
gofmt, go vet, go test, go build
Skip tests when the diff is small
answer: gofmt go vet go test go build
story_fail: A reliable Go ship line formats, analyzes, tests, and builds before release.
explanation: Engineering concept: quality is a system. Repeatable checks catch style drift, suspicious code, broken behavior, and build failures before users do.
